intro: The biggest real split between Slack and Discourse is lock-in posture: Slack carries medium lock-in (data exportable, ecosystem lock), while Discourse carries low lock-in (oss forum, self-hostable). That single fact shapes the rest of the comparison. Slack is built for tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem and is not a fit for microsoft-standardized orgs or teams prioritizing suite-bundled collaboration. Discourse is built for open-source projects and brands wanting indexable, long-form community discussions and is not a fit for teams needing real-time chat or a zero-ops managed experience. On the scoring side, Slack rates 5/5 for developer experience and 5/5 for transparency; Discourse rates 5/5 and 5/5 respectively. The honest trade-off: with Slack you accept that pricing feels punitive at scale, while Discourse comes with the reality that self-hosting ruby stack is operational work. Neither side is free. Slack's headline strength in the data is that default chat for modern knowledge work.
quick_take: Slack is for tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem; Discourse is for open-source projects and brands wanting indexable, long-form community discussions; decide based on.
verdict_a: Choose Slack if you are tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem and can accept medium lock-in (data exportable, ecosystem lock).
verdict_b: Choose Discourse if you are open-source projects and brands wanting indexable, long-form community discussions and need low lock-in (oss forum, self-hostable).

faq.a: Both list a freemium pricing model in our data, and both publish a free tier. We don't have per-seat numbers in this dataset, so 'cheaper' depends on volume. Price both on your actual usage before deciding.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Slack to Discourse?
faq.a: Slack has medium lock-in (data exportable, ecosystem lock); Discourse has low lock-in (oss forum, self-hostable). Migration effort scales with how much state and automation you've built in Slack; plan for data export, config rebuild, and a parallel-run period before cutting over.
Both Slack and Discourse score 5/5 on developer experience in our data, so DX isn't the tiebreaker here. Decide on fit, pricing, or lock-in instead.

faq.q: Is Discourse a good alternative to Slack?
faq.a: Discourse is built for open-source projects and brands wanting indexable, long-form community discussions and explicitly not for teams needing real-time chat or a zero-ops managed experience, so it's a fit only if your workflow matches its stated audience. If you were using Slack for tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem, the switch is reasonable; outside that scope, look elsewhere.
